coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-poly lactic-co-glycolic acid hybrid nanoparticles for chemo-photothermal therapy against bacterial biofilms
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Zheng
Zhou, Tongchang
Yuan, Yuan
Natalie Kłodzińska, Sylvia
Zheng, Tao
Sternberg, Claus
Mørck Nielsen, Hanne
Sun, Yi
Wan, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Bacterial biofilm represents a protected mode of bacterial growth that significantly enhances the resistance to antibiotics. Poly lactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA)-based nanoparticle delivery systems have been intensively investigated to combat the bacterial biofilms-associated infections. However, some drawbacks associated with current PLGA-based nanoformulations (e.g. the relatively low drug loading capability, premature burst release and/or incapability of on-demand release of cargos at the site of action) restrict the transition from the lab research to the clinical applications. One potent strategy to overcome the above-mentioned limitations is exploiting the unique properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and combining CQDs with the conventional PLGA nanoparticles. In the present study, the CQDs were innovatively incorporated into PLGA nanoparticles by using a microfluidic method. The resulting CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles presented good loading capability of azithromycin (a macrolide antibiotic, AZI) and tobramycin (an aminoglycoside antibiotic, TOB), and stimuli-responsive release of the cargos upon laser irradiation. Consequently, AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles showed chemo-photothermally synergistic anti-biofilm effects against P. aeruginosa biofilms. Additionally, the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles demonstrated good biocompatibility with the eukaryotic cells. Overall, the proof-of-concept of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles may open a new possibility in chemo-photothermal therapy against bacterial biofilms.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
PLGA nanoparticles
Chemo-photothermal therapy
Bacterial biofilms
Azithromycin
Tobramycin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles
Preparation and characterization of CQDs-antibiotics composite and antibiotics-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles
Antimicrobial efficacy against P. aeruginosa biofilms

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Teknologi og Produktion, Det Frie Forskningsråd

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic illustration of preparation of nanoparticles by using the microfluidic method; b) size and polydispersity index (PDI) of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles prepared with distinct mass ratios of PLGA and CQDs, control group: PLGA nanoparticles without CQDs; c) zeta potential of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles prepared with distinct mass ratios of PLGA and CQDs, control group: PLGA nanoparticles without CQDs; d) TEM image of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles, scale bar:200 nm; e) encapsulation efficiency (EE) and loading capability (LC) of the CQDs into PLGA nanoparticles prepared with distinct mass ratios of PLGA and CQDs; f) photothermal heating curves of CQDs (red), CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles (black), CQDs-AZI/TOB composites (blue) and PLGA nanoparticles (without CQDs, green) upon 808 nm laser irradiation at 3 W/cm2. Results denote average value ± SD (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Loading capacity (LC, antibiotic mg/mg CQDs) of AZI (a) and TOB (b) into the CQDs.

ATR-FTIR spectra of CQDs (black), AZI (blue), TOB (purple), CQDs-AZI composite (red), CQDs-TOB composite (olive). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

a) Free AZI and TOB release profiles from dialysis bag (black solid line: free AZI, red solid line: free TOB); b) AZI release profiles from CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles (red solid line: formulation A, black solid line: formulation B, blue solid line: formulation C); c) TOB release profiles from CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles (red solid line: formulation D,: black solid line: formulation E,: blue solid line: formulation F); d) NIR-triggered AZI and TOB release profiles from CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles (black solid line: formulation C with laser irritation; black dot line: formulation C without laser irradiation; red solid line: formulation F with laser irritation; red dot line: formulation F without laser irradiation). Sample ID referring to Table 1. Results denote average value ± SD (n = 3). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Confocal images of P. aeruginosa biofilm (PAO1 tagged with GFP) treated with AZI and AZI-loaded hybrid nanoparticle formulation. The viable and dead P. aeruginosa bacteria appear green and red, respectively. Bacterial biofilms were exposed for 4 h to: a) AZI solution; b) AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticle formulation; c) AZI solution and laser irradiated at 2 h; d) AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticle formulation and laser irradiated at 2 h. Scale bars: 20 μm.

Cytotoxic effect of CQDs (a), CQDs-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles, and AZI-loaded CQDs-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles (c) towards HepG2 cells (blue), Calu-3 cells (green), and RAW264.7 (orange). Results denote average value ± SD (n = 6). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Z-average, zeta potential, PDI, and drug loading of antibiotics-loaded CQD-PLGA hydrid nanoparticles. Results denote average value ± SD (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot-poly lactic-co-glycolic acid hybrid nanoparticles for chemo-photothermal therapy against bacterial biofilms

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacy, University of Copenhagen, Universitetsparken 2, DK-2100 Copenhagen Ø, Denmark
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacy, University of Copenhagen, Universitetsparken 2, DK-2100 Copenhagen Ø, Denmark

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Health Technology, Technical University of Denmark, Building 220, Søltofts Plads, DK-2800 Lyngby, Denmark
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Health Technology, Technical University of Denmark, Building 220, Søltofts Plads, DK-2800 Lyngby, Denmark

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Center for Biopharmaceuticals and Biobarriers in Drug Delivery, University of Copenhagen, Universitetsparken 2, DK-2100, Copenhagen Ø, Denmark

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center for Biopharmaceuticals and Biobarriers in Drug Delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Center for Biopharmaceuticals and Biobarriers in Drug Delivery, University of Copenhagen, Universitetsparken 2, DK-2100, Copenhagen Ø, Denmark

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biotechnology and Biomedicine, Technical University of Denmark, Building 221, Søltofts Plads, DK-2800 Lyngby, Denmark
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biotechnology and Biomedicine, Technical University of Denmark, Building 221, Søltofts Plads, DK-2800 Lyngby, Denmark

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Pharmacy, University of Copenhagen, Universitetsparken 2, DK-2100 Copenhagen Ø, Denmark.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial biofilm represents a protected mode of bacterial growth that significantly enhances the resistance to antibiotics. Poly lactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA)-based nanoparticle delivery systems have been intensively investigated to combat the bacterial biofilms-associated infections. However, some drawbacks associated with current PLGA-based nanoformulations (e.g. the relatively low drug loading capability, premature burst release and/or incapability of on-demand release of cargos at the site of action) restrict the transition from the lab research to the clinical applications. One potent strategy to overcome the above-mentioned limitations is exploiting the unique properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and combining CQDs with the conventional PLGA nanoparticles. In the present study, the CQDs were innovatively incorporated into PLGA nanoparticles by using a microfluidic method. The resulting CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles presented good loading capability of azithromycin (a macrolide antibiotic, AZI) and tobramycin (an aminoglycoside antibiotic, TOB), and stimuli-responsive release of the cargos upon laser irradiation. Consequently, AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles showed chemo-photothermally synergistic anti-biofilm effects against P. aeruginosa biofilms. Additionally, the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles demonstrated good biocompatibility with the eukaryotic cells. Overall, the proof-of-concept of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles may open a new possibility in chemo-photothermal therapy against bacterial biofilms.

ce:section.ce:para: More than 60% of bacterial infections in humans worldwide involve the formation of biofilms (e.g. chronic respiratory infections in patients with cystic fibrosis, chronic non-healing wounds, and medical device-related infections) [1]. Formation of biofilm represents a protected mode of growth that allows bacteria to survive in hostile environments (e.g. by becoming 100–1000 times more resistant to antibiotics than planktonic bacteria [2]), leading to numerous treatment failures in the clinic. Nanoparticle-based delivery strategies are employed to combat the bacterial biofilms-associated infections [3,4]. Among these, poly lactic-co-glycolic acid (PLGA, a FDA-approved biodegradable polymer)-based nanoparticle delivery systems have been intensively investigated, owing to the significant advantages of PLGA nanoparticles, such as good biocompatibility, sustained drug release, as well as good stability during preparation and storage [5–7]. Encapsulation of antibiotics into PLGA nanoparticles potentially allows for protection of the antibiotic against degrading enzymes and prevention of inactivation due to binding to components surrounding the biofilm. However, some drawbacks associated with current PLGA-based nanoformulations significantly restrict the transition from in vitro to in vivo studies, thereby impeding clinical applications. Challenges include, among others, the relatively low drug loading capability, premature and/or initial burst release, and incapability of on-demand release of cargos at the site of action [6].

Quantum dots (QDs) have attracted increasing attention in biomedical applications, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, gene delivery, and cancer therapy [8–11]. In particular, combination of QDs with polymeric drug delivery systems may offer the additional advantages. For example, the efforts have been made to hybridize the grapheme quantum dots with polymeric hydrogel to enhance the photothermal behavior of the hydrogel for efficiently trigger the drug release [12]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are proven to possess excellent drug/gene loading capacity due to their large specific surface area and stable interaction with various molecules through π-π stacking, hydrophobic interaction, electrostatic attraction, or physisorption [13,14]. In addition, CQDs have strong absorption at near-infrared (NIR) region and can efficiently convert optical energy into heat, thus offering potential for phototherapy [15,16]. In the present work, we attempted to address the above mentioned limitations of conventional PLGA nanoparticles by exploiting the unique properties of CQDs and combining CQDs with the PLGA nanoparticles. The CQDs with an average size of 4 nm and a zeta potential of −16.2 mV were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis and they possessed –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups on the surface as described previously [17]. The CQDs was incorporated into PLGA nanoparticles by using a microvotex-based microfluidic method that allows for precisely controlling the formation of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles. We demonstrated that CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles presented high antibiotic loading capacity (up to approximately 30%, w/w), good biocompatibility, and excellent photothermal effect that allowing for the stimuli-responsive release of the cargos, and synergic anti-biofilm efficacy against P. aeruginosa biofilms.

Azithromycin dihydrate (≥98%), Tobramycin (≥94%), Heptafluorobutyric acid (HFBA, ≥98%) was purchased from Fisher Scientific (Roskilde, DK). Poly (D, l-lactide-co-glycolide) (lactide: glycolide 50:50, ester terminated) (PLGA RG503, MW: 24000–38000), 4-(2-Hydroxyethyl) piperazine-1′-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), Trifluoroacetic aid (≥99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Broendby, DK). Acetonitrile (HPLC grade), Sodium hypochlorite solution (14%) were purchased from VWR Chemical (Avantor, DK). Ammonium sulfate (≥99%), potassium chloride (≥99%), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (≥99%) and sodium chloride (≥99%) were purchased from Merck Chemical (Darmstadt, DE). Propidium iodide (PI, 1.0 mg/mL solution in water) was purchased from Molecular Probes (Eugene, OR, USA). P. aeruginosa (PAO1 tagged with GFP) were kindly provided by Department of Biotechnology and Biomedicine, Technology University of Denmark. Ultrapure water for sample preparation was obtained from a PURELAB flex machine (ELGA LabWater, High Wycombe, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs with –NH2, –OH, and –COOH functional groups on the surface were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis synthesis as described previously [17]. Citric acid was used as the carbon source to facilitate the dehydration and carbonization, and EDA was applied as a nitrogen-containing precursor and surface passivation agent. The CQDs were characterized with an average size of 4 nm and a zeta potential of −16.2 mV (Fig. S1). CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were prepared by using a microfluidic system composed of a microfluidic chip and two automated pumps (Pump 11 Elite, Harvard Apparatus, Holliston, Massachusetts, USA) as previously reported [18]. Briefly, the organic phase (i.e. CQDs dispersed in PLGA acetonitrile solution) and the aqueous phase (i.e. HEPES buffer (10 mM, pH7.4)) were pumped into the inner channel and the outer channels with the flow rates of 1 mL/min and 5 mL/min, respectively. The resulted nanoparticles dispersion was collected in a beaker with magnetic stirring for 2–3 h to evaporate the organic solvent.

To quantify the encapsulation efficiency (EE%) and loading capacity (LC%) of the CQDs in the nanoparticles, the resulted CQDs-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles dispersion was filtered by using 0.02 μm membrane filter (Whatman, Dassel, DE). The filtered solution containing the unencapsulated CQDs were collected and transferred into a black 96-well plate to measure the fluorescent intensity (λexcitation/λemission = 360/427) by using a microplate reader CLARIOstar PLUS with atmospheric control unit (BMG LABTECH, Ortenberg, DE). A standard curve of the CQDs was made to relate the fluorescent intensity to the concentration of CQDs. EE% and LC% of CQDs in the nanoparticles were calculated by using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively:
(1)
EE % =
A m o u n t
o f
C Q D s
d o s i n g - A m o u n t
o f
t h e
u n e n c a p s u l a t e d
C Q D s
A m o u n t
o f
C Q D s
d o s i n g
× 100 %
(2)
LC % =
A m o u n t
o f
C Q D s
d o s i n g - A m o u n t
o f
t h e
u n e n c a p s u l a t e d
C Q D s
A m o u n t
o f
C Q D s
d o s i n g + a m o u n t
o f
P L G A
d o s i n g
× 100 %

Size and zeta potential of the nanoparticles were measured by using a Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK) equipped with 633 nm laser and 173°detection optics at 25 °C. Each sample of nanoparticles was diluted to 0.1 mg/mL (PLGA concentration) with ultrapure water. Data acquisition and analysis were performed by using the Malvern DTS v6.30 software (Malvern Instruments). The morphology of the nanoparticles was observed by using a transmission electron microscope H-9500 (HITACHI, Deutschland, DE). The concentrated nanoparticles suspension was mounted onto a TEM grid. 1% (w/v) ammonium molybdate was stained for 1 min followed by liquid removed from grid with a filter paper. All of the observations were made in the bright field mode at an acceleration voltage of 10 kV. The digital images were recorded with a Gatan Imaging Filter 100 CCD camera (Gatan, Pleasanton, CA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of CQDs-antibiotics composite and antibiotics-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
Azithromycin or Tobramycin dissolved in the mixture of ethanol and HEPES buffer (10 Mm, pH7.4) (3:7, v/v) was added into the CQDs dispersion (0.2% CQDs in the HEPES buffer, w/v). The mixture solution was covered by aluminum foil and stirred by magnetic overnight. Then, the resulted CQDs-antibiotics composites were washed and purified by using ultra-centrifugal filtration tube (MWCO 100 kDa, Merck, Darmstadt, DE). The CQDs-antibiotics composites in the ultra-centrifugal filtration tube were collected and freeze dried by using a freeze-drier (Epsilon 2-4LSC, Martin Christ, Osterode a.H, DE). The dried powder was stored in a −20 °C freezer for future usage. The content of the antibiotics in the wash-out buffer was determined by using the high-performance liquid chromatography with an evaporative light scattering detector (ELSD-HPLC). The antibiotic loading in the composite was calculated by using the Eq. (3):
(3)
DL
( m g / m g C Q D s )
=
A m o u n t
o f
a n t i b i o t i c s
d o s i n g - A m o u n t
o f
a n t i b i o t i c s
i n
s u p e r n a t a n t
A m o u n t
o f
C Q D s
d o s i n g

For the preparation of the antibiotics-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles, antibiotics-CQDs composites were dispersed into the PLGA organic solution as the organic phase. Then, the antibiotics-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were prepared by using the microfluidic method as previously described. To determine the antibiotic loading, the resulted antibiotics-loaded CQDs-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were transferred into Amicon® filter (MWCO 100 kDa, Merck, Darmstadt, DE) and centrifuged (Thermo Scientific, Heraeus Megafuge 16R) for for 3 min (5000 rpm, 22 °C). The unencapsulated antibiotics in the supernatant were analyzed by using the ELSD-HPLC method. The drug loading (DL %) was calculated by using the Eq. (4):
(4)
D L % =
m
antibiotics
M
antibiotics
+ m
PLGA
× 100 %
where DL% is the loading of antibiotics (AZI and TOB) into nanoparticles; Mantibiotics is the amount of antibiotics (AZI and TOB) in the non-purified nanoparticles dispersion, m antibiotics is the content (mass) of antibiotics (AZI and TOB) in the purified nanoparticles; m PLGA is the content (mass) of PLGA in the purified nanoparticles.

The HPLC analyses were performed using Agilent 1260 Infinity Ⅱ LC (Agilent Tech, Inc, USA), equipped with an autosampler (G1329B), column thermostat (G1316A), binary pump delivery system (G1312B), high performance degasser (G4225A), diode array detector 9G4212B), and an ELS-detector (9G4260B) with nitrogen as carrier gas. Agilent Poroshell 120 EC-C18 column (3.0 × 50 mm, 2.7 μm) was applied as an analytical column. AZI and TOB were eluted using a linear gradient of solvent B (acetonitrile- water- trifluoroacetic acid 90: 10: 0.1, v/v/v) in solvent A (0.2% v/v heptafluorobutyric acid in water) from 10% to 40% over 4 min at a flow rate of 0.6 mL/min. The injection volume was set as 15 μL and column heating temperature was set to 30 °C. Chromatogram were recorded and analysed via Agilent Technologies Open-lab CDS Chem-station software.

One milliliter of CQDs suspension (0.1 mg/mL CQDs in the suspension) was placed in a dialysis device (Float-A-Lyzer, MWCO 100KDa, Merck, Darmstadt, DE) and immersed in 5 mL HEPES buffer solution (10 mM. pH 7.4) at 37 °C with shaking using a linear shaking bath system (GLS400, Grant Instrument, DE) and irradiated by using a fiber-coupled a laser device (MDL-XF-808, Changchun New Industries Optoelectronic Technology Co. Ltd, Changchun, CN) at a power density of 1, 2, and 3 W/cm2. The temperature inside dialysis device was monitored by thermocouple sensor connected with TC-08 Thermocouple Data Logger (Pico Technology, UK) and recorded by PicoLog 6 Beta. The CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles suspension and CQDs-antibiotics composites suspension were investigated using the same method with the CQDs content of 0.1 mg/mL in the suspension.

The in vitro release of AZI and TOB from nanoparticles formulations were investigated using a dialysis method. Briefly, one milliliter of nanoparticles formulations and/or antibiotics solutions as control (0.5 mg/mL antibiotics) were loaded into the dialysis devices (Float-A-Lyzer, MWCO 100KDa, Merck, Darmstadt, DE) and immersed in 5 mL HEPES buffer solution (10 mM. pH 7.4) at 37 °C with shaking using a linear shaking bath system (GLS400, Grant Instrument, DE). The release medium was completely replaced with fresh HEPES buffer solution at each time point (1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 18 h, 24 h, 42 h, 48 h, 65 h and 72 h). To investigate the NIR-triggered release of antibiotics from the nanoparticles, the laser irradiations (λ = 808 nm, 3 min, 3 W/cm2) were performed on the nanoparticle formulations in the dialysis devices at 2 h, 4 h, and 7 h. The laser was generated using a laser device (MDL-XF-808, Changchun New Industries Optoelectronic Technology Co. Ltd, Changchun, CN). The antibiotics concentration was assayed using the ELSD-HPLC method previously described.

Cell culturing: Three different cell lines were used: HepG2, Calu-3 and Raw264.7 (ATCC, Manassas, VA, USA). HepG2 cells were cultured in flat-bottomed 96-well MicroWell™ plates (NUNC, Roskilde, DK) at a seeding density of 10,000 cells/well in EMEM supplemented with penicillin (100 U/mL), streptomycin (100 μg/mL), l-glutamine (2 mM), sodium pyruvate (1 mM), non-essential amino acids (10 mg/mL), and fetal bovine serum (100 mg/mL) under standard culturing conditions (37 °C, 5% CO2, humidified air). Calu-3 cells were cultured in collagen-coated flat-bottomed 96-well MicroWell™ plates (NUNC) at a seeding density of 6000 cells/well in DMEM supplemented with penicillin (100 U/mL), streptomycin (100 μg/mL), l-glutamine (2 mM), and fetal bovine serum (100 mg/mL) under standard culturing conditions (37 °C, 5% CO2, humidified air). RAW 264.7 cells were cultured in flat-bottomed 96-well MicroWell™ plates (NUNC) at a seeding density of 6000 cells/well in DMEM supplemented with penicillin (100 U/mL), streptomycin (100 μg/mL), l-glutamine (2 mM), and fetal bovine serum (100 mg/mL) under standard culturing conditions (37 °C, 5% CO2, humidified air). Cells were cultured until 80–90% confluency was reached, and then washed twice with DMEM prior to use.

Cell viability: Cell viability was determined by the MTS/PMS assay. Quantum dots, PLGA NPs encapsulating quantum dots and PLGA NPs encapsulating quantum dots and azithromycin and were suspended in DMEM to provide isotonic samples and immediately applied to the cell layers and incubated for 1 h. After 1 h of incubation, the samples were removed and the cells were washed Hank’s Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS) buffered with 10 mM HEPES (hHBSS). Subsequently, a mixture of 240 μg/mL MTS and 4.8 μg/mL PMS in hHBSS was applied to the cells. The cells were incubated at 37 °C until an absorbance of ~0.5 for the negative controls was reached, after which the absorbance was measured at 492 nm by using a POLARstar Optima spectrophotometer (GMBLabtech). Absorbance values after incubation of cells with sodium dodecyl sulfate (0.2% (w/v) in hHBSS) defined 100% cell death (positive control, Abspos), while absorbance from of cells incubated with DMEM defined 0% cell death (negative control, Absneg). Cell viability was calculated according to the equation (5). Experiments were performed as two biological replicates (three technical replicates each) on two separate days.
(5)
Viability
%
=
(
Abs
sample
-
Abs
pos
)
(
Abs
neg
-
Abs
pos
)
× 100 %

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial efficacy against P. aeruginosa biofilms
ce:section.ce:para: Cultivation: Biofilms of P. aeruginosa were grown in a flow-chamber system containing individual channels with the dimensions of 1 × 4 × 40 mm. 200 µL aliquots of overnight cultures of P. aeruginosa (tagged with green fluorescent protein) were inoculated in each channel of the flow chamber after pre-grown in LB medium at 37 °C under vigorous shaking and adjusted to an optical density of 0.001 at 600 nm by using 0.9% NaCl solution. After inoculation, the flow channels were left without flow for 1 h at 37 °C to allow adhesion of the bacterial cells to the substratum. And the growth medium was supplied at a flow rate of 3 mL/h by using a peristaltic pump (Watson-Marlow 205S, Watson-Marlow, Cornwall, UK). The flow-chamber system was incubated at 37 °C for 3–4 days until the biofilms were ready for the following investigation. The design of the flow chamber and the growth medium were described in our previous study [19,20].

Killing efficacy: To investigate the killing efficacy, each channel containing P. aeruginosa biofilms was gently dosed with 200 µL aliquots of concentrated AZI-loaded nanoparticles suspension or AZI solution (corresponding to a total of 0.5 mg AZI), after which the system was left without flow for 30 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, the 3 mL/h flow of the growth medium was resumed. To differentiate dead and live cells, propidium iodide (PI, dead cell indicator) was added to the medium at a final concentration of 0.3 mM. The laser irradiation (λ = 808 nm, 2 min, 3 W/cm2) was given at 2 h. then, the biofilms were imaged at 4 h using a confocal laser scanning microscope (DMi8 S Platform, Leica Microsystem, DE) equipped with the detectors and filter sets for monitoring of GFP (excitation, 488 nm; emission, 517 nm) and propidium iodide (PI, excitation, 535 nm; emission, 617 nm). Images were obtained using a 20×/1.3 Plan-Neofluar dry immersion objective. Simulated 3D images were generated using the Leica Application Suits X (Leica Microsystem, DE).

The CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were prepared by using a microfluidic method as previously reported [18], with the mass ratio of PLGA and CQDs at a range from 10:1 to 200:1 (w/w) to investigate the encapsulation efficacy of CQDs into PLGA nanoparticles and optimize the formulation parameters (Fig. 1 a). Size and zeta potential of the resulting CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were in the range of 100 to 150 nm and −20 to −50 mV, respectively (Fig. 1b, c). The TEM image confirmed the ultrasmall size with the narrow distribution (Fig. 1d). In addition, the encapsulation efficacy and the loading capacity of the CQDs into PLGA nanoparticles could be tuned by varying the mass ratio of PLGA to CQDs. For example, the encapsulation efficacy of the CQDs into PLGA nanoparticles was observed to be about 75% at the mass ratio of 10 (PLGA:CQD = 10:1, w/w), which resulted in a loading capacity of approximately 7.5% (w/w) (i.e. 0.075 mg CQDs/1 mg PLGA nanoparticles) (Fig. 1e). In comparison, at the mass ratio of 20 (PLGA:CQDs = 20:1, w/w), about 90% of the CQDs was encapsulated into the PLGA nanoparticles, leading to about 4.3% (w/w) of loading capacity (Fig. 1e). This tunable encapsulation efficacy and loading capacity of the CQDs into the PLGA nanoparticles may provide adequate space for optimizing the photothermal effect of the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles and minimizing possible toxicity induced by the CQDs.

The photothermal properties of the CQDs and the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were investigated by 808 nm laser irradiation at different power densities. The temperature of the CQDs suspension (0.1 mg/mL) rapidly increased from 37 °C to approximately 43 °C upon laser irradiation at the intensity of 3 W/cm2. However, the temperature of the PLGA nanoparticles (without CQDs) suspension showed no increase upon the laser irradiation. In contrast, the temperature of the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles suspension (with the CQDs content of 0.1 mg/mL in the suspension) increased from 37 °C to approximately 41 °C upon the laser irradiation at the intensity of 3 W/cm2 (Fig. 1f). Additionally, the increases in temperature were observed to accomplish within 60 s and the prolongation of the irradiation time appeared no further increase in temperature. It indicates that the CQDs encapsulated in the PLGA nanoparticles can rapidly and efficiently convert NIR light into thermal energy. Additionally, the increase in the CQDs content resulted in enhanced photothermal effects (Fig. S2).

The anti-biofilm efficacy of the antibiotic-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles was investigated by using P. aeruginosa biofilms grown in a flow-chamber system under well-controlled and hydrodynamic conditions [20]. P. aeruginosa biofilms preformed in the flow-chamber system were treated with AZI aqueous solution and AZI-loaded nanoparticle formulations with or without laser irradiation, followed by staining with propidium iodide (PI, specifically stain the dead cells [26] without obvious influence on the preformed P. aeruginosa biofilms [19]). Finally, P. aeruginosa biofilms were imaged by using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) and the killing efficacy was evaluated by using the fluorescence intensity (red color) of dead bacterial cells in the CLSM images. To improve the penetration of the nanoparticles into the biofilms and prolong the contact time between the bacteria and the nanoparticle formulation, the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles were further coated with a TPGS shell as reported in our previous study [19] (which resulted in the nanoparticle formulation with the average size of 119.6 nm, zeta potential of −26.3 mV, and AZI loading of 17% (PLGA:CQD:AZI = 50:3.3:8.25, w/w)) and employed to treat the preformed P. aeruginosa biofilms. As observed in the CLSM images after 4 h, the P. aeruginosa biofilms treated with the AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles presented more dead bacterial cells (red) than those treated with the free AZI (Fig. 5 a, b). This enhanced killing efficacy likely results from nanoparticle-mediated site-specific delivery of AZI, consequently resulting in increased antibiotic concentration in close proximity to the biofilm-forming bacteria [19]. In addition, it was observed that, given the laser irradiation (2 min, 3 W/cm2) at 2 h, the P. aeruginosa biofilms treated with both the AZI solution and the AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles showed more dead bacteria than those in the absence of NIR irradiation (Fig. 5c, d). In contrast, the CQDs and the laser irradiation (2 min at 3 W/cm2) showed subtle effect on the preformed P. aeruginosa biofilms (Fig. S4). These observations clearly indicate the synergistic anti-biofilm efficacy induced by the chemo-photothermal effect. In particular, the P. aeruginosa biofilms treated with the AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles showed more dead bacteria than those treated with the AZI solution, indicating the enhanced killing efficacy induced by the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles. The enhanced killing efficacy could be mainly attributed to: i) the on-demand release induced by laser irradiation, which increases the local concentration of AZI; and ii) the photothermal property of the AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles could increase the permeability of the bacterial membrane [27].

At last, the potential effects of the CQDs and the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles on the viability of eukaryotic cells were assessed in macrophages (RAW264.7), lung epithelial cells (Calu-3), and hepatocytes (HepG2). The IC50 values of the CQDs on RAW264.7, Calu-3, and HepG2 were approximately 3.48, 3.81 and 4.95 mg/mL, respectively (Fig. 6 a). These results suggest low cytotoxicity of the CQDs on the eukaryotic cells. Furthermore, by controlling the CQDs content in the nanoparticle formulation (approximately 10% CQD to PLGA, w/w), both the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles and the AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles presented good compatibility towards RAW264.7, Calu-3, and HepG2 (Fig. 6b, c).

The present work demonstrates that the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles may represent a safe and effective formulation approach for combined chemo-photothermal therapy against bacterial biofilms. The limitations (e.g. the relatively low drug loading capability, premature burst release and/or incapability of on-demand release of cargos at the site of action) of conventional PLGA nanoparticles significantly impede the transition from lab research to clinical application. Inspired by the unique properties of CQDs (e.g. excellent drug loading capacity and possibility of the stimuli-responsive release of the payloads upon laser irradiation), we attempted to synthesize the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles by using a microfluidic method to overcome the limitations of conventional PLGA nanoparticles and seek for the synergistic anti-biofilm efficacy induced by the chemo-photothermal effect. In comparison with the conventional PLGA nanoparticles reported in our previous study [19], the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles significantly improve the antibiotic loading capacity and decrease the premature burst release. Furthermore, the CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles can rapidly and efficiently convert NIR light into thermal energy, leading to an increase in the temperature of the nanoparticles suspension from 37 to 41 °C. The photothermal effect allows for i) the stimuli-responsive release of the payloads by disrupting the nanoscale network of nanoparticles [24,25]; and ii) increasing the permeability of the bacterial membrane [27]. Benefited from the features, the AZI-loaded CQD-PLGA hybrid nanoparticles showed the synergistic anti-biofilm effect against P. aeruginosa biofilms. In the future, it will be relevant to 1) optimize the surface passivation of CQDs for further improvement of the drug loading capacity; 2) investigate the relationship between the stimuli-responsive release pattern and the anti-biofilm efficacy, and 3) evaluate the therapeutic efficacy of the optimal formulation(s) using in vivo model of chronic non-healing wounds.

Zheng Huang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Tongchang Zhou: Methodology, Investigation. Yuan Yuan: Methodology, Investigation. Sylvia Natalie Kłodzińska: Methodology, Investigation. Tao Zheng: Methodology, Investigation. Claus Sternberg: Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Hanne Mørck Nielsen: Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Yi Sun: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Feng Wan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: F.W. acknowledges the financial supports from the Danish Council for Independent Research, Technology and Production Sciences (DFF–4093-00062) and the Lundbeck Foundation (R264-2017-3404). Y.S. acknowledges the financial supports from the Villum Fonden (project no. 13153).
